questions.question: What is Linear Blend Skinning (LBS)?
options: Weighted blend of bone transforms per vertex: v' = Σ w_i·M_i·IB_i·v. Standard GPU skinning.
questions.correctAnswer: Weighted blend of bone transforms per vertex: v' = Σ w_i·M_i·IB_i·v. Standard GPU skinning.
questions.explanation: Each vertex influenced by 1-4 bones. Bone weights sum to 1. GPU blends 4 matrices per vertex. Fast but causes candy-wrapper effect.

questions.question: What is the candy-wrapper effect in LBS?
options: When a joint twists, linear blend of rotation matrices produces non-rigid transforms — mesh collapses inward at joint
questions.correctAnswer: When a joint twists, linear blend of rotation matrices produces non-rigid transforms — mesh collapses inward at joint
questions.explanation: Cause: SO(3) not closed under addition. Blending two rotation matrices does NOT produce a rotation matrix. Determinant < 1 = volume collapse.

questions.question: How do dual quaternions fix the candy-wrapper effect?
options: Dual quaternion blend + normalization always produces rigid transform — rotation+translation, determinant=1, no volume loss
questions.correctAnswer: Dual quaternion blend + normalization always produces rigid transform — rotation+translation, determinant=1, no volume loss
questions.explanation: DQS: convert bone transforms to dual quaternions, blend, normalize. Result always rigid. ~2× cost of LBS. UE supports both.

questions.question: What is the bind pose?
options: The skeleton pose the mesh was modeled in — the reference configuration for skinning
questions.correctAnswer: The skeleton pose the mesh was modeled in — the reference configuration for skinning
questions.explanation: Bind pose = rest pose. Inverse bind matrices (IB_i) map vertices to bone-local space IN the bind pose. Computed once at import.

questions.question: How many bones typically influence a single vertex?
options: 4 (with MAX_BONE_INFLUENCES). Weights sum to 1. Extra influences dropped if > limit.
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (with MAX_BONE_INFLUENCES). Weights sum to 1. Extra influences dropped if > limit.
questions.explanation: UE default: 4 influences per vertex. 8 possible with higher quality settings. Artists paint weights in Maya/Blender. Red=strong, blue=weak.

questions.question: What does the Fourier Transform convert?
options: Time-domain signal to frequency-domain — reveals which frequencies (pitches) are present
questions.correctAnswer: Time-domain signal to frequency-domain — reveals which frequencies (pitches) are present
questions.explanation: DFT: X[k] = Σx[n]·e^(-i·2π·k·n/N). Maps N time samples to N frequency bins. Magnitude = amplitude, angle = phase.

questions.question: Why is the FFT O(N log N) instead of O(N²)?
options: Cooley-Tukey: decomposes DFT of size N into two DFTs of size N/2 recursively — divide and conquer
questions.correctAnswer: Cooley-Tukey: decomposes DFT of size N into two DFTs of size N/2 recursively — divide and conquer
questions.explanation: X[k] = E[k] + W_N^k·O[k]; X[k+N/2] = E[k] - W_N^k·O[k]. Butterfly operations. log₂(N) stages. N=1024: DFT=1M ops, FFT=10K ops.

questions.question: How does the convolution theorem enable fast reverb?
options: Time convolution = frequency multiplication. FFT both signals, multiply, IFFT result. O(N log N) vs O(N²) direct.
questions.correctAnswer: Time convolution = frequency multiplication. FFT both signals, multiply, IFFT result. O(N log N) vs O(N²) direct.
questions.explanation: Dry audio ∗ impulse response → FFT(audio)·FFT(IR) → IFFT. 10,000× faster for real-time convolution reverb.

questions.question: What is a spectrogram?
options: 2D visualization: time on x-axis, frequency on y-axis, color = magnitude. Generated by sliding FFT window.
questions.correctAnswer: 2D visualization: time on x-axis, frequency on y-axis, color = magnitude. Generated by sliding FFT window.
questions.explanation: Spectrogram = STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform). Window function (Hann) reduces leakage. Hop size controls time resolution. Used in audio editors.

questions.q: What window function is commonly used before FFT and why?
o: Hann window — tapers signal to zero at edges, reducing spectral leakage from abrupt frame boundaries
questions.a: Hann window — tapers signal to zero at edges, reducing spectral leakage from abrupt frame boundaries
questions.e: Window functions prevent spectral leakage from the discontinuity at frame boundaries. Hann, Hamming, Blackman are common choices.

questions.question: How does sound intensity fall off with distance?
options: Inverse-square law: I = P/(4πr²). Every distance doubling = -6 dB.
questions.correctAnswer: Inverse-square law: I = P/(4πr²). Every distance doubling = -6 dB.
questions.explanation: SPL(r) = SPL(1m) - 20·log₁₀(r). At 1m=80dB, 2m=74dB, 4m=68dB. Games clamp with min/max distance and configurable falloff.

questions.question: What cues does the brain use to localize sound?
questions.correctAnswer: ITD (interaural time difference), ILD (interaural level difference), and spectral notches (pinna filtering)
acceptableAnswers: time difference, level difference, pinna filtering
questions.explanation: ITD: ~0.25ms max. ILD: head shadow attenuates far ear (high freqs). Spectral notches: pinna shape → frequency dips that encode elevation.

questions.question: What is an HRTF?
options: Head-Related Transfer Function — pair of filters per direction capturing how sound reaches eardrums. Convolution applies spatialization.
questions.correctAnswer: Head-Related Transfer Function — pair of filters per direction capturing how sound reaches eardrums. Convolution applies spatialization.
questions.explanation: HRTF(θ,φ) = FIR filter (128-512 taps) per ear. Measured in anechoic chamber. Applied via convolution (FFT-based). Enables 3D audio on headphones.

questions.question: What causes the Doppler effect for sound?
options: Relative motion: f_heard = f_source·c/(c+v_relative). Approaching = higher pitch. Receding = lower.
questions.correctAnswer: Relative motion: f_heard = f_source·c/(c+v_relative). Approaching = higher pitch. Receding = lower.
questions.explanation: c = ~343 m/s. Car at 30 m/s approaching: f_heard = f_source·1.096 (~1.6 semitones higher). UE applies per-sound-source.

o: L=cos(θ+90°), R=sin(θ+90°). Prevents center from being +3dB louder than sides.
questions.a: L=cos(θ+90°), R=sin(θ+90°). Prevents center from being +3dB louder than sides.
questions.e: Two speakers at center = +3dB if linear. Constant-power: -3dB each at center = combined 0dB. Total power constant across pan positions.

questions.question: Why is interpolation needed for networked multiplayer?
options: Server updates arrive at 10-20Hz (50-100ms apart). Client renders at 60Hz — must interpolate between received positions.
questions.correctAnswer: Server updates arrive at 10-20Hz (50-100ms apart). Client renders at 60Hz — must interpolate between received positions.
questions.explanation: Without interpolation: characters teleport between received positions. With: smooth motion, ~50ms render-behind latency (acceptable for other players).

questions.question: What is client-side prediction?
options: Client simulates own input locally for instant response, then reconciles with server's authoritative state
questions.correctAnswer: Client simulates own input locally for instant response, then reconciles with server's authoritative state
questions.explanation: Own character: can't wait 50ms RTT. Predict locally → send input → server corrects → rewind and replay. UE's CharacterMovementComponent does this.

questions.question: What is dead reckoning?
options: Extrapolating motion when packets stop arriving — assume constant velocity/acceleration
questions.correctAnswer: Extrapolating motion when packets stop arriving — assume constant velocity/acceleration
questions.explanation: Dead reckoning: last position + velocity·dt (+ ½·acceleration·dt²). After timeout (1-2s), freeze or disconnect. Used for packet loss.

questions.question: What is a jitter buffer?
options: Queue that delays packet playback to smooth network jitter — trades added latency for smooth motion
questions.correctAnswer: Queue that delays packet playback to smooth network jitter — trades added latency for smooth motion
questions.explanation: Packets arrive with variable delay. Buffer holds and releases at steady rate. Larger buffer = smoother + more latency. Adaptive buffers resize.

questions.q: What is Hermite interpolation advantage over linear for networking?
o: Uses endpoint velocities for C¹ continuity — no sudden direction changes at received updates
questions.a: Uses endpoint velocities for C¹ continuity — no sudden direction changes at received updates
questions.e: Hermite: position + velocity at each endpoint. Produces smooth curves. Linear: constant velocity between updates, jerky direction changes.
